ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Caesar salad; A0CKM (mixed vegetable salad with dressing) is the closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Caesar salad as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Caesar salad is approximately 200 g, comprising a generous bowl of romaine lettuce, Caesar dressing, Parmesan shavings, and croutons.

nutrient_source.title: Caesar salad — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Caesar salad (cr_id G00010758), confidence 0.95. All per-100g values are preserved from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed green salad with dressing (GI ~15)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Caesar salad in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on the closest analogous entry: mixed green salad with oil-based dressing, which is dominated by non-starchy vegetables and fat. Carbohydrate per serving is very low (5.9 g/100g × 200 g serving = 11.8 g), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 1.8 per serving. This is consistent with published GI values for low-carbohydrate salad dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; driven by anchovy-based Caesar dressing, Parmesan cheese, and Worcestershire sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Caesar salad; if consumed, request dressing on the side and use sparingly, and avoid additional Parmesan or croutons to reduce sodium intake below 600 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (5.9 g/100g) with very high fat (44.54 g/100g) slows gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose rise, but caloric density is very high at 430 kcal/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Caesar salad is conditionally suitable for Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate load and low GI (~15), but the very high fat content and caloric density require portion control; request without croutons and verify dressing for added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at ~7.2 g/100g from mayonnaise-based dressing and Parmesan, combined with sodium at 810 mg/100g and cholesterol at 26 mg/100g, presents a compounded cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Caesar salad due to high saturated fat, high sodium, and elevated cholesterol; substitute with an olive-oil-and-lemon dressing and omit croutons to reduce atherogenic and hypertensive load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~7.2 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; zero dietary fibre provides no anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Caesar salad is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis due to high saturated fat from the dressing and Parmesan, which promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; substitute with an olive oil and lemon dressing and add anti-inflammatory vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Caesar salad is a classic Western salad dish originating in Tijuana, Mexico, popularised across the United States and now served globally. It consists of crisp romaine lettuce leaves tossed in a rich Caesar dressing made from anchovies, egg yolk, garlic, lemon juice, Worcestershire sauce, and olive oil or mayonnaise, topped with shaved or grated Parmesan cheese and crunchy croutons. The flavour profile is bold, savoury, and umami-rich, with a creamy, tangy dressing coating each leaf. The texture contrasts soft lettuce with crunchy croutons and firm Parmesan shavings. Nutritionally, Caesar salad is very high in fat (44.54 g/100g), predominantly from the dressing, and very high in sodium (810 mg/100g), making it unsuitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease without modification. Carbohydrate content is low at 5.9 g/100g, giving it a low glycemic index (~15) and minimal glycemic load per serving (~1.8), which is conditionally suitable for Type 2 Diabetes. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 7.2 g/100g, which is a concern for both cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0.0 g/100g. In Singapore, Caesar salad is commonly served as a starter or light meal at Western restaurants and cafes. The input record contained a misspelling ('Ceaser Salad') and an incorrect cuisine attribution ('Indian'), both of which have been corrected in this record.
common_user_queries: Is Caesar salad good for diabetes?
Is Caesar salad high in sodium?
Can I eat Caesar salad with high blood pressure?
Is Caesar salad suitable for heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010758) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from known composition of Caesar dressing components (mayonnaise, Parmesan) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this single field is medium confidence. All other values are exact HPB matches.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this Caesar salad preparation; romaine lettuce fibre is minimal and the dressing-heavy formulation yields negligible fibre at the reported composition. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this Caesar salad preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026. 3. saturated_fat_g (7.2 g/100g) is estimated from the known composition of Caesar dressing components (mayonnaise base, Parmesan cheese, egg yolk) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central values for these ingredients; this is an adapted estimate, not a direct HPB measurement. 4. Input record contained a misspelling ('Ceaser Salad') corrected to 'Caesar Salad', and an incorrect cuisine attribution ('Indian') corrected to 'American / Western'. 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on mixed green salad with oil-based dressing from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct Caesar salad GI measurement is available in approved sources. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (15) × carbohydrate per serving (11.8 g) / 100 = 1.77, rounded to 1.8.

Added saturated_fat_g (7.2) estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for Caesar dressing ingredients.
Added GI (15, low) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database with full documentation.
Added glycemic_load_per_serving (1.8) calculated from GI and carbohydrate per serving.

Documented all -1 fields and the dietary_fibre_g = 0.0 confirmed zero in notes.
